message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest
DIMENSION: ops, packaging & supply chain
CLAIMED SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: pyproject.toml 7
DESCRIPTION: `pyproject.toml` sets `version = "0.0.1"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth — hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions["engine"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A release that bumps only one of the two strings (the common failure mode) ships a wheel whose distributed version disagrees with the version recorded in every provenance manifest it produces — silently undermining the project's central reproducibility guarantee while passing all tests.
SUGGESTED FIX: Single-source the version. Either: (a) set `dynamic = ["version"]` in pyproject and add `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"` so hatchling reads `__version__` from the module; or (b) drop the literal from `__init__.py` and derive it at runtime via `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")`. Option (a) keeps the determinism manifest source authoritative and removes the drift surface.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	[build-system]
2	requires = ["hatchling"]
3	build-backend = "hatchling.build"
4	
5	[project]
6	name = "assay-engine"
7	version = "0.0.1"
8	description = "Dataset-agnostic engine for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data."
9	readme = "README.md"
10	requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"
11	license = "Apache-2.0"
12	license-files = ["LICENSE"]
13	authors = [{ name = "[REDACTED]" }]
14	keywords = ["empirical", "reproducibility", "methodology", "security", "privacy", "ml", "nlp"]
15	classifiers = [
16	    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
17	    "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
18	    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
19	    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
20	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
21	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
22	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
23	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
24	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
25	    "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering",
26	    "Topic :: Security",
27	    "Typing :: Typed",
28	]
29	# The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are optional extras (lazy-imported,
30	# ADR-0006). The composed runner and methodology work with no extras installed.
31	dependencies = []
32	
33	[project.urls]
34	Homepage = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay"
35	Repository = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay"
36	Issues = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/issues"
37	
38	[project.optional-dependencies]
39	dev = [
40	    "pytest>=8",
41	    "ruff>=0.6",
42	    "mypy>=1.11",
43	]
44	# Heavy backends for the reasoning seam — installed only where real LLM calls run.
45	# The engine imports and unit-tests (mocking the backends) without these.
46	reasoning = [
47	    "langchain-ollama>=1.1,<2",
48	    "claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3",
49	    "anyio>=4,<5",
50	]
51	observability = [
52	    "opentelemetry-sdk>=1.25,<2",
53	    "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp>=1.25,<2",
54	    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",
55	    "openinference-instrumentation-langchain>=0.1.40,<1",
56	    "mlflow>=3.1,<4",
57	]
58	persistence = [
59	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",  # core — provides MemorySaver (declared, not left transitive)
60	    "langgraph-checkpoint-postgres>=3.1,<4",
61	    "psycopg[binary,pool]>=3.2,<4",
62	    "qdrant-client>=1.9,<2",
63	]
64	orchestration = [
65	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",
66	]
67	baseline = [
68	    "numpy>=1.26,<3",  # vectorized similarity at corpus scale (pure-Python fallback otherwise)
69	]
70	# Every backend in one shot (for a full local install). dev tooling stays separate.
71	all = [
72	    "assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]",
73	]
74	
75	[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
76	packages = ["src/assay_engine"]
77	
78	[tool.pytest.ini_options]
79	testpaths = ["tests"]
80	pythonpath = ["src"]
81	
82	[tool.ruff]
83	line-length = 100
84	src = ["src", "tests"]
85	
86	[tool.mypy]
87	python_version = "3.11"
88	packages = ["assay_engine"]
89	strict = true
90	
91	# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
92	# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
93	[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
94	module = [
95	    "langchain_ollama.*",
96	    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
97	    "anyio.*",
98	    "opentelemetry.*",
99	    "phoenix.*",
100	    "mlflow.*",
101	    "openinference.*",
102	    "langgraph.*",
103	    "qdrant_client.*",
104	    "psycopg.*",
105	    "psycopg_pool.*",
106	    "numpy.*",
107	]
108	ignore_missing_imports = true
109

1	"""assay_engine — the reusable, dataset-agnostic engine of the hc-assay blueprint.
2	
3	The single architectural rule (ADR-0002, ARCHITECTURE.md §3):
4	
5	    The engine never imports dataset specifics. Adapters implement the interfaces in
6	    ``assay_engine.contracts`` that the engine calls. A clone is: implement the adapter,
7	    register it, run.
8	
9	This package contains only goal-agnostic machinery:
10	
11	- ``contracts``      — the engine ↔ adapter boundary (canonical schema + adapter Protocols).
12	- ``methodology``    — hypotheses, the three verdicts, the two firewalls, the
13	                       measurement↔interpretation fence, confirmatory-test machinery.
14	- ``baseline``       — dataset-agnostic baseline builders (embeddings, similarity, graph,
15	                       clustering, descriptive stats).
16	- ``orchestration``  — the analysis graph, phase machine, and governance gates.
17	- ``reasoning``      — the tiered LLM reasoning seam.
18	- ``observability``  — self-hosted tracing + experiment tracking.
19	- ``persistence``    — durable checkpointing, data versioning, vector store.
20	- ``provenance``     — the append-only, hash-chained audit trail (GOVERNANCE §3).
21	- ``pipeline``       — ``run_study``: the composed, governed end-to-end runner (ADR-0010).
22	- ``registry``       — adapter registration.
23	
24	Nothing in this package may import an adapter module or name a specific dataset, authority,
25	or taxonomy.
26	"""
27	
28	__version__ = "0.0.1"
29	
30	# Public API — the surface a cloning study uses. Heavy/optional backends stay behind their
31	# subpackages (imported lazily there); these names are dependency-free to import.
32	from assay_engine.contracts import (  # noqa: E402
33	    ClaimRecord,
34	    Corpus,
35	    ExternalClaimsSource,
36	    FeatureBuilder,
37	    FeatureMatrix,
38	    IngestionParser,
39	    Relation,
40	    StudyDefinition,
41	    StudyMode,
42	    Unit,
43	)
44	from assay_engine.pipeline import (  # noqa: E402
45	    GateHandler,
46	    GateReview,
47	    IngestionError,
48	    StudyPlan,
49	    StudyResult,
50	    auto_approve,
51	    run_study,
52	)
53	from assay_engine.provenance import (  # noqa: E402
54	    ProvenanceEntry,
55	    ProvenanceError,
56	    ProvenanceTrail,
57	    from_records,
58	    verify_records,
59	)
60	from assay_engine.registry import (  # noqa: E402
61	    get_study,
62	    register_study,
63	    registered_studies,
64	)
65	
66	__all__ = [
67	    "__version__",
68	    # contracts
69	    "ClaimRecord",
70	    "Corpus",
71	    "ExternalClaimsSource",
72	    "FeatureBuilder",
73	    "FeatureMatrix",
74	    "IngestionParser",
75	    "Relation",
76	    "StudyDefinition",
77	    "StudyMode",
78	    "Unit",
79	    # pipeline
80	    "GateHandler",
81	    "GateReview",
82	    "IngestionError",
83	    "StudyPlan",
84	    "StudyResult",
85	    "auto_approve",
86	    "run_study",
87	    # provenance
88	    "ProvenanceEntry",
89	    "ProvenanceError",
90	    "ProvenanceTrail",
91	    "from_records",
92	    "verify_records",
93	    # registry
94	    "get_study",
95	    "register_study",
96	    "registered_studies",
97	]
98

1	"""Determinism & reproducibility harness for the baseline (ADR-0001, METHODOLOGY.md §1, §7).
2	
3	A baseline is only a yardstick if the *same inputs reproduce the same baseline*. This module
4	makes that contract concrete and engine-level: it content-hashes inputs, derives seeds
5	deterministically from those hashes, records component versions, and stamps all of it into a
6	:class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineArtifact`'s ``determinism`` record — so a
7	hostile reviewer can confirm a baseline was produced exactly as claimed.
8	
9	It is dependency-free and pure. The heavy, *choice-bearing* builders (which embedding model,
10	which clustering algorithm, which graph construction) encode dataset/study decisions and are
11	supplied by a study's adapter as :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`
12	implementations — not prescribed by the engine (ADR-0002). This harness records *how* such a
13	builder ran so the result is reproducible regardless of which builder it was.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import hashlib
19	import json
20	import sys
21	from dataclasses import dataclass
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION
25	
26	# The canonical hashing primitives moved to ``assay_engine._canonical`` (see note below); these
27	# imports re-export them at their historical home so existing call sites keep working.
28	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_plain as _plain  # noqa: F401
29	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_bytes as hash_bytes  # noqa: F401  (re-export)
30	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_text
31	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_value as hash_value  # re-export (baseline public API)
32	from assay_engine._canonical import keytag as _keytag  # noqa: F401
33	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
34	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
35	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
36	
37	# The canonical hashing primitives (``_plain``/``_keytag``/``hash_bytes``/``hash_text``/
38	# ``hash_value``) live in ``assay_engine._canonical`` so they sit below both ``baseline`` and
39	# ``methodology`` in the import graph (audit #B1/#B2/#B5 originally landed here; hoisted so
40	# pre-registration can content-bind a hypothesis with the *same* type-faithful digest). They are
41	# re-exported above for back-compat.
42	
43	
44	def corpus_fingerprint(corpus: Corpus) -> str:
45	    """Deterministic content hash of a corpus (order-independent across units/relations)."""
46	    units = [
47	        {"id": u.unit_id, "text": u.text, "attrs": _plain(u.attributes)}
48	        for u in sorted(corpus.units, key=lambda u: u.unit_id)
49	    ]
50	    relations = [
51	        {"s": r.source_id, "t": r.target_id, "k": r.kind, "attrs": _plain(r.attributes)}
52	        for r in sorted(corpus.relations, key=lambda r: (r.source_id, r.target_id, r.kind))
53	    ]
54	    payload = {"units": units, "relations": relations, "metadata": _plain(corpus.metadata)}
55	    return hash_text(json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False))
56	
57	
58	def stable_seed(*parts: str, bits: int = 32) -> int:
59	    """A deterministic non-negative integer seed derived from ``parts``.
60	
61	    Derived from a hash so the same inputs always yield the same seed — reproducible without a
62	    hard-coded magic number, and distinct inputs get distinct seeds.
63	    """
64	    digest = hashlib.sha256("\x1f".join(parts).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
65	    return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)
66	
67	
68	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
69	class DeterminismRecord:
70	    """The reproducibility provenance stamped onto a baseline (ADR-0001)."""
71	
72	    seed: int
73	    input_hashes: Mapping[str, str]
74	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        object.__setattr__(self, "input_hashes", freeze_mapping(self.input_hashes))
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "component_versions", freeze_mapping(self.component_versions))
79	
80	    def as_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
81	        return {
82	            "seed": self.seed,
83	            "input_hashes": dict(self.input_hashes),
84	            "component_versions": dict(self.component_versions),
85	        }
86	
87	
88	def build_baseline_artifact(
89	    corpus: Corpus,
90	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
91	    *,
92	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
93	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
94	    seed: int | None = None,
95	) -> BaselineArtifact:
96	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
97	
98	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
99	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
100	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
101	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
102	    so it is reproducible.
103	    """
104	    corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
105	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
106	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
107	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
108	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
109	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
110	
111	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
112	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
113	
114	    if seed is None:
115	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
116	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
117	
118	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
119	    return BaselineArtifact(
120	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
121	    )
122

content.text: The core claims are accurate. Let me verify the "no dynamic versioning" claim and test the actual behavior in the venv.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -c "
import assay_engine
print('module __version__:', assay_engine.__version__)
try:
    from importlib.metadata import version
    print('installed dist version:', version('assay-engine'))
except Exception as e:
    print('dist lookup error:', e)
"
input.description: Compare module version vs installed dist version

content.text: Confirmed: no `dynamic`/`hatch.version` config exists, so the two strings are genuinely independent. Both currently agree at 0.0.1. Let me demonstrate the drift scenario concretely to gauge real impact.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -c "
from importlib.metadata import version, distribution
print('install mode:')
import assay_engine, os
print(' module file:', assay_engine.__file__)
# editable install? check if importing from src
print(' from src:', 'src' in assay_engine.__file__)
print()
# Simulate: if a release bumps pyproject only, the determinism manifest still uses __init__.__version__
from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import _ENGINE_VERSION
print('determinism manifest engine version source:', _ENGINE_VERSION)
print('this is __init__.__version__, NOT the dist metadata version')
"
input.description: Confirm determinism manifest uses module version, not dist metadata

content.content:
install mode:
 module file: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/__init__.py
 from src: True

determinism manifest engine version source: 0.0.1
this is __init__.__version__, NOT the dist metadata version

content.text: I have enough to assess. Let me check whether there's any release tooling/CI that would catch drift, which would mitigate severity.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rln "version" .github/ Makefile* scripts/ tools/ 2>/dev/null | head -20; echo "---grep version-check/consistency---"; grep -rn "__version__\|pyproject" tests/ .github/ 2>/dev/null | grep -i "version" | head
input.description: Check for CI/test guard against version drift

content.text:
No CI or test guard exists to catch version drift between the two strings. My verification is complete.

The finding is accurate on all factual claims:
- `pyproject.toml:7` hardcodes `version = "0.0.1"`
- `src/assay_engine/__init__.py:28` independently hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"`
- No `dynamic`/`[tool.hatch.version]` config exists (confirmed by grep)
- `determinism.py:24` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` and `:111` stamps it into `component_versions["engine"]` in the reproducibility/provenance record
- No CI or test asserts the two stay in sync

This is a genuine single-source-of-truth defect with a real (if conditional) reproducibility consequence. Severity calibration: it does not currently produce wrong output (both agree), there's no metered/network angle, and it only manifests on a future partial version bump — a real but latent maintenance hazard. The "central reproducibility guarantee" framing is somewhat overstated because the provenance record is a self-consistent internal artifact (it records whatever the running module reports), so a drift wouldn't corrupt the hash chain or make a baseline irreproducible — it would only make the recorded engine label disagree with the distributed wheel's metadata label. That's a provenance-accuracy/auditability issue, not a correctness break. LOW-to-MEDIUM; I land on LOW given it requires a future human error to trigger and has a trivial, well-known fix.

input.reasoning:
The factual claims are all verified against the real code. The package version is hardcoded in two independent places with no single source of truth: pyproject.toml line 7 (`version = "0.0.1"`) and src/assay_engine/__init__.py line 28 (`__version__ = "0.0.1"`). I confirmed via grep that there is NO dynamic versioning configured — no `dynamic = ["version"]` and no `[tool.hatch.version]` section in pyproject.toml (the only `__version__`/`dynamic` hits are in __init__.py itself). So the two strings are genuinely decoupled and a release that bumps only one will drift.

The provenance link is also real: src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py line 24 imports `from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION`, and line 111 stamps it as `component_versions["engine"]` into the DeterminismRecord that is embedded in every BaselineArtifact's determinism record. I confirmed at runtime in the venv that this value is the module's `__version__`, not the distribution metadata version.

I also checked for any mitigating guard: there is no CI workflow, Makefile, test, or script that asserts the two version strings stay in sync (grep over .github/, tests/, scripts/, tools/ found nothing). So the common failure mode (bump one, forget the other) is unguarded and would pass all tests.

I down-calibrate from the claimed MEDIUM to LOW for two reasons. (1) It is currently latent — both strings agree at 0.0.1, so there is no present incorrect behavior; it only manifests on a future human partial-bump. (2) The reporter's framing that drift "silently undermines the central reproducibility guarantee" is overstated: the determinism/provenance record is internally self-consistent (it records whatever the running module reports, and the hash chain is over that recorded value), so a drift does NOT corrupt the hash chain or make a baseline non-reproducible. The concrete harm is narrower — the engine-version *label* recorded in provenance manifests would disagree with the distributed wheel's metadata version, an auditability/provenance-accuracy blemish, not a correctness or reproducibility break. That, plus the requirement of a future human error to trigger and the trivial well-known fix, places it at LOW. None of the project's hard constraints (local-first, no metered API, engine-never-imports-adapters, lazy heavy backends) are implicated, so this is not excused as a justified design choice.

content.text: Verification complete. The finding is real but I recalibrated severity from MEDIUM to LOW. All factual claims check out (two independent hardcoded version strings, no dynamic versioning config, the second feeds the provenance manifest, no CI/test guard). However, the harm is latent (both agree today, requires a future partial bump to trigger) and narrower than claimed — drift would mislabel the engine version in provenance manifests but would NOT corrupt the hash chain or break reproducibility, since the determinism record is internally self-consistent. The suggested fix (single-source via `[tool.hatch.version]`) is sound.
